coredata.dc:title: Flexible self-supporting electrode for high removal performance of arsenic by capacitive deionization
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Bin
Wang, Ranran
Fan, Yamin
Li, Bei
Zhang, Jie
Peng, Fuquan
Du, Yi
Yang, Wenzhong
coredata.dc:description: Capacitive deionization (CDI) technology has promising applications for the removal of arsenic from micro polluted water. The selection of electrode materials and the fabrication of electrodes are the core parts of the CDI technology. In this paper, the structure of graphene was modulated by inserting carbon quantum dots to form three-dimensional (3-D) rGO/NCDs electrode material and electrospinning method was applied to fabricate monolithic mesh self-supporting electrodes with excellent electrochemical and structure properties. In the experiments of arsenic removal, the rGO/NCDs electrode achieved an electrosorption capacity of 15.07 mg g−1 at 1.0 V, increasing by 29.5% compared with that of rGO electrode. In addition, the model fitting analysis of the experimental data showed that arsenic removal was consistent with pseudo-first-order kinetic model and Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The thermodynamic model suggested that electrosorption was a spontaneous exothermic process. The intraparticle diffusion model further revealed the diffusion mechanism of arsenic removal by CDI electrodes. In conclusion, the prepared flexible self-supporting rGO/NCDs electrode with high electrosorption capacity provided a new idea for the electrode preparation process and further promoted the large-scale application of CDI technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrosorption
Graphene
Capacitive deionization
Arsenic removal

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the prepared electrodes: (a) rGO, (b) rGO/NCDs.

CV curves of the prepared electrodes: (a) rGO, (b) rGO/NCDs. (c) Specific capacitance of the prepared electrodes. Cyclic GCD curves of the prepared electrodes: (d) rGO, (e) rGO/NCDs. (f) The 20th cycle GCD curves of the prepared electrodes at 1 A g−1.

Plots of SAC vs. t of the prepared electrodes in a 5 mg L-1 arsenic solution: (a) rGO, (b) rGO/NCDs. CDI Ragone plots of SAR vs. SAC of the prepared electrodes in a 5 mg L-1 arsenic solution: (c) rGO, (d) rGO/NCDs.

Electrosorption capacities of the prepared electrodes at different flow rate (a), initial concentration (b), temperature (c) and electrode distance (d). (e) Regenerated adsorption capacities of the prepared electrodes. (f) Energy consumption and charge efficiency of the prepared rGO/NCDs electrode for arsenic removal.
Electrosorption behavior of As(III) on the prepared electrodes fitted by adsorption isotherms: (a) Freundlich, (b) Langmuir.
Electrosorption behavior of As(III) on the prepared electrodes fitted by kinetics models: pseudo-first-order model (a, b), pseudo-second-order model (c, d).
The thermodynamic equation fitting of the prepared electrodes: (a) rGO, (b) rGO/NCDs for arsenic removal.
Intraparticle diffusion model fitting of the prepared electrodes: (a) rGO, (b) rGO/NCDs for arsenic removal.
Characterization of rGO/NCDs electrode during arsenic removal: (a) XPS full spectra, high-resolution spectra for As3d (b) and N1s (c,d).
BET results of rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode.
Parameters determined by fitting of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms for CDI performance of rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode.
Parameters determined by fitting of electrosorption kinetic models for CDI performance of rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode.
The thermodynamic parameters of rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode for electrosorption of arsenic.
Intraparticle diffusion model parameters of rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode for electrosorption of arsenic.

head.ce:title: Flexible self-supporting electrode for high removal performance of arsenic by capacitive deionization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Yangjing Environmental Protection Service Co., Ltd, Lian Yungang 222000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Capacitive deionization (CDI) technology has promising applications for the removal of arsenic from micro polluted water. The selection of electrode materials and the fabrication of electrodes are the core parts of the CDI technology. In this paper, the structure of graphene was modulated by inserting carbon quantum dots to form three-dimensional (3-D) rGO/NCDs electrode material and electrospinning method was applied to fabricate monolithic mesh self-supporting electrodes with excellent electrochemical and structure properties. In the experiments of arsenic removal, the rGO/NCDs electrode achieved an electrosorption capacity of 15.07 mg g−1 at 1.0 V, increasing by 29.5% compared with that of rGO electrode. In addition, the model fitting analysis of the experimental data showed that arsenic removal was consistent with pseudo-first-order kinetic model and Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The thermodynamic model suggested that electrosorption was a spontaneous exothermic process. The intraparticle diffusion model further revealed the diffusion mechanism of arsenic removal by CDI electrodes. In conclusion, the prepared flexible self-supporting rGO/NCDs electrode with high electrosorption capacity provided a new idea for the electrode preparation process and further promoted the large-scale application of CDI technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Arsenic, as a highly biological toxic element, is considered to be one of the most serious inorganic pollutants in the contamination of water resources [1]. Long-term consumption of water with high arsenic content will cause chronic poisoning, posing a great threat to human health [2,3]. In addition, excess arsenic inhibits plant’s growth by altering the selective permeability of cells and preventing the uptake of nutrients [4]. The problem of arsenic contamination has aroused widespread concern and the maximum concentration of arsenic decreased from 50 µg/L to 10 µg/L according to drinking water standards [5]. Hence, it is necessary to remove the harmful arsenic contamination before discharging into the environment. At present, the traditional arsenic removal methods mainly include oxidative precipitation [6], flocculation and filtration [7], ion exchange [8], membrane separation [9] and reverse osmosis [10], but they have many disadvantages such as energy-extensive consumption, poor efficiency, high cost and easy to produce pollution. In contrast, capacitive deionization (CDI) technology has a very broad application prospect in the field of water treatment because of its easy operation, low power consumption, no secondary pollution and high desalination efficiency [11,12].

CDI has received increasing attention because it can be not only a desalination technique but also a novel method for heavy metal removal [13]. The selection of electrode materials and the electrode manufacturing method are the crucial parts. At present, there are two main types of CDI electrode materials: one is conductive non-Faraday materials, such as carbon materials. It is based on the principle of electric double-layer capacitance for the purpose of removing heavy metal ions. The other is redox-active/Faradaic materials, including electrochemical ion exchange electrodes materials and ion intercalation electrodes materials [14,15]. During the electrosorption process, redox-active/Faradaic materials can capture specific heavy metal ions by redox reactions with significant selective ion separation [16]. Compared with redox-active/Faradaic materials, carbon materials can have better conductivity, cycling stability and active material utilization [14,17]. The ideal carbon material requires a large specific surface area (SSA), excellent electrical conductivity and a well-developed pore structure [18–20]. Graphene is considered as the suitable material due to its unique two-dimensional nanostructure, huge SSA and ultra-high electrical conductivity [21,22]. However, simple graphene inevitably generates lamellar overlap in the preparation process, resulting in a decrease in its SSA utilization, which has an adverse effect on the capacitive performance of CDI electrodes [23]. Carbon quantum dots are a kind of novel 0-D carbon material with a particle size below 10 nm, which is emerging as a new star in the carbon nanomaterial family due to its excellent chemical inertness, water solubility, low toxicity and electron transport properties [24–27]. Furthermore, introducing N atoms on the surface of carbon quantum dots can accelerate the electron transport and transfer properties. Therefore, the 3-D spatial structure composite formed by inserting N doped carbon quantum dots into graphene can deliver higher SSA and more active sites, which in turn improves the electrosorption capacity.

The conventional preparation method of CDI electrode is to mix electrode materials, conductive agents and binders [28,29]. However, the electrodes prepared by this method generally apply hydrophobic binder, such as polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and other organic materials, which have poor electrical conductivity and easily block the pores of the electrode material. In recent years, electrospinning has received widespread attention for its simple operational process, environmental friendliness and ability to prepare flexible, macroscopic monolithic meshes for use in capacitors, adsorbents and catalyst carriers [30–34]. Due to these unique properties, electrospinning has great potential to produce flexible self-supporting CDI electrodes on a large scale.

Herein, we demonstrated the feasibility of CDI for arsenic removal from micro polluted water. The prepared CDI electrodes with a monolithic mesh structure were innovatively electrospun using rGO/NCDs composites as raw material. The arsenic removal performance was systematically investigated under different influencing factors and the suitable electrosorption condition was obtained. Based on the experimental results, adsorption models and elemental valence/bonding changes were further evaluated to probe the adsorption mechanism of arsenic removal. This work showed that rGO/NCDs self-supporting electrode had the potential to elevate the deionization performance and the obtained basic parameters can be expanded to further large-scale CDI applications.

Polyamindoamine dendrimer was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The potassium permanganate, sulfuric acid (98%), hydrochloric acid (37%), hydrogen peroxide (30%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), ethanol, sodium arsenite (NaAsO2) and N, N-dimethylformamide were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Citric acid anhydrous and polyacrylonitrile were offered by Aladdin and J&K Scientific Co. Ltd., respectively. All solutions in this work were prepared using deionised water and all purchased materials were not purified in any way.

Graphene oxide (GO) was obtained using a reported modified Hummers method [35]. The NCDs were produced with polyamindoamine dendrimer and citric acid by a hydrothermal method [36]. The preparation steps of rGO/NCDs composite were as follows. First, 100 mL GO suspension (2 mg mL−1) and 0.05 g NCDs were mixed, and continued stirring for 1 h. Next, the mixture was poured into an autoclave and heated at a temperature of 180 ℃ for 12 h. Finally, rGO/NCDs composites were obtained by centrifugation, washing, and freeze-drying at −40 ℃. rGO was synthesized without NCDs powder in the same steps. The surface morphologies of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites were shown in Fig. S1. In the further electrode fabrication process, the spinning solution was produced by dissolving polyacrylonitrile (PAN) with a certain amount of active electrode materials (rGO or rGO/NCDs) in dimethylformamide solution at 60 ℃ under stirring conditions. Using tin foil as a collector, the nanofiber flexible self-supporting electrode was obtained by adjusting key parameters such as electrospinning voltage of 18 kV, distance between collector and spinneret of 15 cm and syringe pump rate of 1 mL h−1 at room temperature (25 ℃). The prepared electrodes were assembled into symmetrical CDI unit modules without any additives for subsequent experimental studies.

The surface morphology of rGO and rGO/NCDs electrode fibers were characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, regulus 8100). The SSA and pore structure were measured using a specific surface and porosity analyser (BET, Mac2460). The functional groups on the electrode materials were identified by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR, Vertex 70, Bruker). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) was used to characterize the elemental valence states. The electrochemical properties of the electrodes were investigated by a three-electrode formulation in which 20 mg L-1 NaAsO2, platinum foil and saturated calomel electrode were employed as the electrolyte, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The electrochemical experiments were all studied using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua). For cyclic voltammetry (CV), the different scan rates were performed in the voltage range of −0.4 V to 0.6 V. For constant current galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests, a current density of 1 A g−1 was set. For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the experiments were conducted in a peak voltage excitation of 5 mV with the frequency range of 100 mHz to 100 kHz.

The arsenic removal experiments were carried out by a batch mode experimental configuration. The CDI circulation system consisted of a CDI cell module, a peristaltic pump, an external power supply and a reservoir. Before the beginning of the electrosorption experiments, the CDI electrodes were cleaned with deionized water to remove impurities and then immersed in 5 mg/L of sodium arsenite solution overnight to minimize the impact of physical adsorption. In each experiment, a given potential was applied to the CDI cell and the feed solution (80 mL) was continuously circulated through the CDI unit cell by a peristaltic pump until the arsenic concentration remained constant. The desorption process was realized via a reverse polarity method for about 30 min. The arsenic in the feed solution were continuously removed cycle by cycle. The effects of applied voltage, initial concentration, temperature, flow rate, and electrode distance on the arsenic removal were investigated separately, and each of which was repeated three times. It was worth noting that the electrode distance was changed by increasing and decreasing the number of silicone spacers between the two electrodes. The thickness of one silicone spacer in the middle of the electrodes was 1.5 mm. When setting the electrode distance to 1.5, 3.0, and 4.5 mm, the number of silicone spacers to be placed between the two electrodes was 1, 2, and 3, respectively. Furthermore, the regeneration of CDI electrode was measured by applying a reverse voltage, and the stability of the electrode was studied for 10 cycles. An atomic absorption spectrophotometer was used to measure arsenic concentration. The electrosorption capacity (SAC), electrosorption rate (SAR) and removal efficiency (η) were calculated via the following equations:
(1)
SAC =
(
C 0
-
C e
) V
m
(2)
SAR =
SAC
t
(3)
η =
C 0
-
C e
C 0
× 100 %
where C e and C 0 are the equilibrated and initial concentrations of arsenic, respectively (mg L-1), m represents the total mass of the active material (0.18 ± 0.005 g), V is the feed solution volume (80 mL), t is the time corresponding to the SAC during the CDI process (min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: CV, GCD and EIS tests were effective methods for evaluating the electrochemical properties of electrodes. Fig. 2 a and 2b depicted the CV curves of rGO and rGO/NCDs electrodes at different scan rates, which displayed good symmetry and no obvious redox peaks. This phenomenon indicated that the electrochemical process was reversible with double layer capacitance characteristics [37]. Compared to the rGO electrode, the specific capacitance (C s) of rGO/NCDs electrode was larger at each scan rate (Fig. 2c), suggesting that the addition of NCDs retarded the agglomeration phenomenon of graphene and increased the electron transfer capacity. Furthermore, C s values of the electrode decreased as the sweep rate increased. This was because the lower the sweep rate, the longer the electrochemical reaction time and thus the more metal ions in the electrolyte solution were adsorbed to form a double layer capacitance [38]. Fig. 2d and 2e displayed the GCD curves for the electrodes cycled 20 times at 1 A g−1, respectively. No platforms were shown in the GCD curves, suggesting no significant redox reaction occurred. The shapes of GCD curves remained highly similar with good reproducibility, proving excellent electrochemical stability [39]. Meanwhile, the charge and discharge time for rGO/NCDs electrode were longer than rGO electrode (Fig. 2f), corresponding to better capacitive performance. To further assess the electrochemical behavior, the EIS images (Fig. S2) of rGO and rGO/NCDs electrodes were measured. The Nyquist plots were similar with an approximate semicircle and a straight line at high frequency and low frequency, respectively, which related to the charge transfer resistance and Warburg impedance (Zw) [40,41]. According to the EIS curves, it was evident that the diameter of the semicircle was smaller and the slope of the straight line was larger of rGO/NCDs electrode, compared to rGO electrode, suggesting that rGO/NCDs electrode had a faster ion transfer rate, a smaller charge transfer resistance, and better capacitive performance, which were conducive to the improvement of the electrosorption rate.
In order to systematically assess the effects of operating conditions on As(III) removal, batch mode CDI experiments were carried out.

In electrosorption research, voltage is a critical influencing factor. The SAC plots of rGO and rGO/NCDs electrodes for As(III) removal at different voltage were shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, respectively. It can be observed that SAC increased fast within the first 20 min, while remained stable after 40 min with the adsorption being saturated. The SAC values increased with increasing voltage, indicating the enhanced Coulomb interactions and the formation of thicker double layer [42,43]. With a voltage of 1.0 V, the SAC of rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode reached 11.64 mg g−1 and 15.07 mg g−1, respectively, demonstrating that the doping of NCDs can improve the pore structure and increase the active sites of graphene to enhance the adsorption performance. CDI Ragone plots were given in Fig. 3c and 3d. The plots shifted to the right and upwards as voltage increased, indicating an increase in both SAC and SAR. This suggested that larger voltage generated stronger electrostatic forces and higher desalination rate, which led to the generation of tighter electric double layers and enhanced ion adsorption capacity [44].

Flow rate mainly affects the stability of the electrosorption system. At each flow rate, rGO/NCDs electrode performed higher SAC values than rGO electrode (Fig. 4 a), indicating that the addition of NCDs provided the graphene electrode a more stable electrical double layer structure. At the same time, the SAC values slowly decreased as the flow rate increased, with the maximum value at 20 mL min−1. High flow rate affected the residence time of adsorbed ions and some ions were washed away before a stable electric double layer formed, so in practice, a suitable flow rate should be selected [45].

Fig. 4b exhibited the SAC of As(III) at different initial concentrations for rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode. It can be seen that the electrosorption of As(III) increased with increasing initial concentration. Higher initial concentration provided more opportunity for the electrode material to come into contact with the ions and greater incentive for diffusion, because of the larger concentration gradient difference [46]. In contrast, it can be observed from Fig. S3 that the η value of As(III) removal decreased with the increase in initial concentration. This fact was due to that the increase of the initial concentration was greater than that of electrosorption capacity [47].

Fig. 4c depicted the SAC of As(III) at different solution temperature for rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode. When the solution temperature reached 40 °C, the SAC values of As(III) for rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode were 9.13 mg g−1 and 12.62 mg g−1, respectively, which were about 27.5% and 19.49% lower than those at 20 °C, respectively. The main reason was that higher temperature led to stronger intermolecular interaction force in the solution, while the electrostatic force between the ions and the electrode remained unchanged, and thus the double electric layer stability became worse. The stored charge subsequently reduced, thus causing a decrease in SAC. In summary, low temperature favored CDI behavior.

The electrode distance affected the thickness of the double electric layer. As the electrode distance increased, the SAC of As(III) firstly increased and then decreased (Fig. 4d). The maximum SAC reached at the electrode distance of 3.0 mm. When the electrode distance was too small, CDI system tended to be short-circuiting, which affected the thickness of the double electric layer. And when the electrode distance was too large, the strength of electric driving force became small and the ion diffusion resistance became large. Therefore, the distance between the electrode plates should be reasonably modulated within the suitable range in actual CDI operation.

The cyclic regenerative electrosorption performance is an important basis for measuring the stability and practicability of electrode. The regeneration performances of electrodes over 10 cycles were investigated under optimal conditions (voltage 1.0 V, flow rate 20 mL min−1, initial concentration 5.0 mg L-1, temperature 20 °C and electrode distance 3.0 mm), respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 4e that the SAC of electrodes first increased and then decreased as cyclic adsorption and desorption proceeded. This may be due to the fact that the active components in the electrode were gradually activated during the electrosorption and desorption in the early stage, resulting in a slow increase in the SAC. In the later stage, the regenerative adsorption of the CDI electrode slowly decreased, probably because some adsorbed ions entered the small pores and micropores of the electrode material, which were difficult to be removed [48–50]. The electrosorption capacity retentions were 89.27% and 93.24% after 10 cycles, respectively, manifesting that both rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode performed excellent cycling and regeneration behaviors.

In addition, a comparison on arsenic removal by CDI method was made between this study and other works, as listed in Table S1 [5,51–59]. It was obvious that the SAC of rGO/NCDs electrode was higher than others, which derived from the unique 3-D flexible self-supporting structure formed by coupling rGO and NCDs. The abundant active sites, suitable porous structure, 'plane-to-linear' conductive network, and without hydrophobic binder led to the superior CDI performance.

In addition to the SAC, the essential parameters such as charge efficiency (Λ) and energy consumption (EEC) are also used for the evaluation of the CDI electrosorption process. The equations were listed as follows [60,61]:
(4)
Λ =
Γ × F
Σ
(5)
Σ =
∫
I d t
m
(6)
EEC =
E ×
∫ 0 t
Idt
V × (
C 0
-
C e
)
where Γ represents the number of ions removed per unit mass of electrode (mol g−1), F is the Faraday constant (96485C mol−1), Σ is the charge transferred per unit mass of electrode (C g−1), I and E are the current (A) and applied voltage (V), respectively.

In the process of electrosorption, the actual value of Λ is less than the theoretical value (100%), which is affected by the resistance of the electrode itself, the transmission resistance between the electrode and the solution interfaces, and common ion effect. As illustrated in Fig. 4f, the values of Λ and EEC increased with the increasing applied voltage in micro-pollution (5 mg L-1) arsenic solution. The Λ of the electrode to remove arsenic could reach 39.41% at 1.0 V. As the voltage increased, the adsorption/desorption ability of arsenic ions on rGO/NCDs electrode was enhanced and more energy was used to adsorb/desorb ions, causing an increase in EEC. At 1.0 V, the EEC of rGO/NCDs electrode for the removal of arsenic was 1.42 J mg−1, lower than the results reported in the literature [61–63].

To investigate the isothermal adsorption behavior of electrodes for arsenic removal, experimental data were simulated using the Langmuir isotherm and Freundlich isotherm, which were listed as follows [64]:
(7)
Langmuir :
C e
q e
=
1
q m
K L
+
C e
q m
(8)
Freundlich : ln
q e
= ln
K F
+
1 n
ln
C e
where q e and q m are the equilibrium and maximum adsorption capacities (mg g−1). K L and K F are the Langmuir and Freundlich constant (L mg−1), respectively. 1/n represents the adsorption tendency of arsenic.

The electrosorption isotherms for rGO and rGO/NCDs electrodes were illustrated in Fig. 5 . The relevant parameters of the fitted results were listed in Table 2 . It was clear that the R 2 values of Langmuir for both rGO and rGO/NCDs electrodes were higher than that of Freundlich, demonstrating that single molecular layer adsorption was dominant. Meanwhile, it was found that 1/n value was<1, suggesting that both electrodes were susceptible to arsenic adsorption [65].

In order to describe the adsorption kinetic behavior of the two CDI electrodes, an adsorption kinetic model was applied to fit the experimental data. It is generally agreed that pseudo-first-order model is dominated by physical adsorption, and pseudo-second-order model is dominated by chemisorption. And their equations were listed below [66,67]:
(9)
Pseudo - f i r s t - o r d e r
m o d e l :
q t
=
q e
-
q e
e
-
K 1
t
(10)
Pseudo - s e c o n d - o r d e r
m o d e l :
t
q t
=
1
K 2
q e 2
+
t
q e

where q e is adsorption capacity (mg g−1) at equilibrium, q t is adsorption capacity (mg g−1) at t (min), K 1 is pseudo-first-order rate constant (min−1), K 2 is pseudo-second-order rate constant (g (mg min)-1).

The fitted kinetic models for arsenic removal by electrosorption were shown in Fig. 6 . The relevant parameters of the fitted results were shown in Table 3 . Higher applied voltage led to higher equilibrium adsorption capacity. According to the correlation coefficients (R 2) values, both adsorption kinetic models can be used to simulate the experimental data. Due to the smaller difference between measured and theoretical q e values, the pseudo-first-order model fitted better with a higher correlation, which was related to physical adsorption.

In order to reveal whether the electrosorption process is spontaneous, exothermic or endothermic, thermodynamic parameters such as standard enthalpy change (ΔH), standard Gibbs free energy changes (ΔG) and the standard entropy change (ΔS) were obtained by equations (11), (12) and (13) [68–70]:
(11)
Δ G =
-
R T lnK
(12)
K =
q e
C e
(13)
lnK =
Δ S
R
-
Δ H
RT
where T is the absolute temperature (K), R represents the ideal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), and K means the adsorption partition coefficient.

Fig. 7 presented the fitting of thermodynamic model to the experimental data at different temperature and the calculated values were listed in Table 4 . The magnitude of ΔG indicates whether the nature of the electrosorption is physical or chemical. The range of ΔG value for the former is −20 ∼ 0 kJ mol−1, and for the latter is −400 ∼ -80 kJ mol−1 [71]. It was clear from Table 4 that ΔG values for both electrodes were negative, and ΔG values ranged from −3.69 to −2.17 kJ mol−1, indicating that arsenic removal process by the electrosorption was a spontaneous physisorption process. This result was consistent with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model analysis [72]. In addition, ΔH < 0 indicated that the electrosorption process was an exothermic reaction and that low temperature was favorable for arsenic removal. ΔS < 0 indicated a decrease in confusion during the electrosorption process, which was due to the migration of arsenic from the feed solution to the electrode surface.

Based on Weber and Morris theory, the intraparticle diffusion model was utilized to determine the diffusion mechanism during the migration of adsorbate from solution to the particle surface [73], and the diffusion model was studied based on the following equation:
(14)
q t
=
k p
t
0.5
+ c
where k p represents the intraparticle diffusion rate constant (mg g−1 min−1/2), q t is adsorption capacity (mg g−1) at t (min), and c is the intercept.

As shown in Fig. 8 and Table 5 , the electrosorption of arsenic on rGO and rGO/NCDs electrodes can be recognized as three stages. The first stage with a high slope was related to the process of rapid diffusion of arsenic from the feed solution to the electrode, which was controlled by the external conductive resistance. The second stage explained the internal diffusion adsorption process where arsenic diffused into the inner pore from the outer surface, and adsorbed on the active sites within the pore. While the third stage indicated the adsorption towards equilibrium. In addition, the k p values tended to increase in the initial linear phase as the applied voltage increased, which was due to a greater electrostatic force. After a careful observation of Fig. 8, it was found that the initial fitted curve intercept didn’t pass through the origin, indicating that internal diffusion was not the only adsorption controlling step. The adsorption process was also influenced by external diffusion or exchange of related substances etc. and the adsorption mechanism was more complex [74].

The FT-IR spectra of rGO/NCDs electrode before and after the adsorption of arsenic were plotted in Fig. S4. After adsorption, the intensities of the peaks at 2500–3500 cm−1 corresponding to O-H, C-H and N-H stretching vibrations were significantly weakened. The peak of N-H stretching vibration at 1617 cm−1 showed a positive shift (36 cm−1) after arsenic adsorption and the peak at 1472 cm−1 disappeared. This indicated that the protonated groups on the electrode acted as Lewis acid sites to form arsenic complexes [75]. Furthermore, the intensities of C-O-C (1055 cm−1), C-O (1153–1229 cm−1), and C-N (1382–1407 cm−1) peaks were all weakened. All these results suggested that amine groups and oxygen-containing groups should be involved in the arsenic adsorption, which would be conductive to the oxidation of As(III) to As (V) [76–78].

In order to better investigate the adsorption mechanism of arsenic removal, surface element species and their valence states of rGO electrode (Fig. S5) and rGO/NCDs electrode (Fig. 9 ) were characterized using XPS. In Fig. 9a, the As 3d peaks were observed at binding energy around 44 eV, indicating the successful adsorption of arsenic ion on the electrode surface under CDI process. Moreover, the high-resolution spectra of As 3d included two peaks (Fig. 9b) [5,58]. Thereinto, the peaks at 44.66 eV and 41.11 eV corresponded to As(V) and As(III), respectively. And the percentage of the peak area of As(III) (22.69%) was smaller than that of As(V) (77.31%), which demonstrated that part of the high poisonous As(III) was oxidized to the low toxic As(V) during the electrosorption process, so both As(V) and As(III) adsorbed on the electrode [53,79]. The N1s spectra before and after adsorption were illustrated in Fig. 9(c, d). After As electrosorption, the binding energies of pyridinic-N (399.06 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.6 eV) and graphitic-N (400.06 eV) peaks changed slightly. However, the signal intensity and relative proportion of pyridinic-N increased obviously. In addition, Fig. S6 depicted the O1s spectra and three peaks corresponding to C-O (531.9 eV), O-H (533.6 eV) and O = C-O (533.1 eV) were found. The signal intensity of O-H decreased after arsenic adsorption, while the signal intensity of O in the lattice increased. In general, it can be speculated that the negative arsenate/arsenite species can form complexes with the empty orbitals above the nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups, thus achieving the removal of arsenic [5,78]. So based on FT-IR and XPS analysis, it was confirmed that nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups were involved in the arsenic electrosorption. Furthermore, XPS tests revealed that the ratio of oxygen content in the electrode material increased from 9.26% to 10.57% after the removal of arsenic by electrosorption (Table S2), further illustrating that the electrosorption process was associated with a slight oxidation reaction.

In summary, we proved that the CDI technique is viable and efficient for arsenic removal from micro polluted water. rGO electrode and rGO/NCDs electrode with monolithic mesh structure were prepared by electrospinning with good electrochemical stability and capacitive performance. In the arsenic removal experiments, the operating conditions all had significant effects on the CDI electrosorption performance. The SAC of rGO/NCDs electrode reached 15.07 mg g−1 at 1.0 V, much higher than those of rGO electrode and other reported electrodes. Furthermore, the arsenic adsorption by rGO/NCDs electrode conformed to pseudo-first-order model and Langmuir isotherm model, which was a homogeneous adsorption behavior of a single molecular layer dominated by physical adsorption. The thermodynamic model suggested that arsenic electrosorption was a spontaneous exothermic process. The intraparticle diffusion model further revealed the diffusion mechanism of arsenic removal. Overall, the developed CDI flexible self-supporting electrode had great application prospects in the field of electrosorption treatment, which can further advance the large-scale development of CDI technology.

Bin Xu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ranran Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Resources. Yamin Fan: Formal analysis, Resources. Bei Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jie Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology. Fuquan Peng: Validation. Yi Du: Validation. Wenzhong Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
